# Ethoko Congiguration Reference

The configuration file is a JSON file that defines the settings for Ethoko.

Ethoko CLI will look for a `ethoko.json` file in the current working directory, and if not found, it will look for parent directories until it finds one or reaches the root directory. The configuration file can also be specified explicitly using the `--config` flag when running Ethoko CLI commands.


```json
{
  "project": "my-project",
  "compilationOutputPath": "./out",
  "storage": {
    "type": "aws",
    "bucketName": "my-ethoko-bucket",
    "region": "us-east-1"
  },
  "debug": false
}
```

## Project and artifacts configuration

- `project`: The name of the project. It is used by default in the CLI commands to identify with wich project to interact with, some CLI commands allow to override it with the `--project` flag.
- `compilationOutputPath`: The path where the compilation artifacts generated by your development environment are located, e.g. `artifacts` for Hardhat projects, `out` for Foundry. It is used by default in the CLI commands to identify where to find the compilation artifacts, the `push` and `diff` CLI commands allow to override it with the `--artifact-path` flag.
- `pulledArtifactsPath`: The path where the pulled artifacts by Ethoko CLI commands are stored locally. It is recommended to add this directory to `.gitignore` to avoid committing the pulled artifacts to the repository. By default, it is set to `.ethoko`.
- `typingsPath`: The path where the TypeScript typings generated by Ethoko CLI commands are stored locally. It is recommended to add this directory to `.gitignore` to avoid committing the generated typings to the repository. By default, it is set to `.ethoko-typings`.
- `storage`: The storage configuration for Ethoko, which defines where the compilation artifacts are stored. See the [Storage configuration](#storage-configuration) section below for more details.
- `debug`: Enables debug mode for Ethoko CLI commands. By default, it is set to `false`.


## Storage configuration

Ethoko supports AWS S3 and local filesystem storage providers.

### AWS S3

Compilation artifacts are stored in an [AWS S3 bucket](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/).


Before using Ethoko with AWS S3, create an S3 bucket and make sure AWS credentials are available. By default, Ethoko uses the AWS SDK's default credential provider chain, which checks the following sources in order:

- Environment variables (`AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`)
- AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO)
- Shared credentials file (`~/.aws/credentials`)
- Shared config file (`~/.aws/config`)
- ECS container credentials
- EC2 instance metadata (IAM roles)

The configuration requires:

- `awsRegion`: AWS region where the S3 bucket is located
- `awsBucketName`: Name of the S3 bucket


Default configuration example:
```json
{
  "project": "my-project",
  "storage": {
    "type": "aws",
    "awsRegion": "us-east-1",
    "awsBucketName": "my-ethoko-bucket"
  }
}
```


Regarding credentials, it is recommended to rely on the default credential provider chain and configure credentials using environment variables or AWS config/credentials files. This allows for better security practices and flexibility across different environments.

If working with a dedicated AWS profile, use the environment variable `AWS_PROFILE` or specify the profile name in the configuration:

```bash
# ~/.aws/config
[profile my-ethoko-profile]
region = eu-west-3
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/my-ethoko-role
source_profile = my-ethoko-source-profile

# ~/.aws/credentials
[my-ethoko-source-profile]
aws_access_key_id = YOUR_ACCESS_KEY_ID
aws_secret_access_key = YOUR_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
```


```json
{
  "project": "my-project",
  "storage": {
    "type": "aws",
    "awsRegion": "us-east-1",
    "awsBucketName": "my-ethoko-bucket",
    "awsProfile": "my-ethoko-profile"
  }
}
```

or set the environment variable:

```bash
AWS_PROFILE=my-ethoko-profile ethoko pull
```

Alternatively, provide explicit static credentials (for example in CI):


```json
{
  "project": "my-project",
  "storage": {
    "type": "aws",
    "awsRegion": "us-east-1",
    "awsBucketName": "my-ethoko-bucket",
    "awsAccessKeyId": "your-access-key-id",
    "awsSecretAccessKey": "your-secret-access-key"
  }
}
```


Optionally, in the case of static credentials, you can assume an IAM role using explicit credentials:

- `awsRoleArn`: ARN of the IAM role to assume
- `awsRoleExternalId`: Optional external ID for cross-account role assumption
- `awsRoleSessionName`: Optional role session name (default: `ethoko-hardhat-session`)
- `awsRoleDurationSeconds`: Optional session duration in seconds (900-43200)

When `awsRoleArn` is provided, Ethoko assumes the role using the access key and secret key, and uses the temporary credentials for S3 operations. The credentials are cached in memory for the duration of the task.

When `awsAccessKeyId` and `awsSecretAccessKey` are omitted, Ethoko relies on the default credential provider chain.

If you want role assumption while still relying on the default credential chain, configure it in your AWS config file using `role_arn` and `source_profile`.

It is possible to use a single bucket for multiple projects, Ethoko will handle the organization of the artifacts within the bucket.

### Local filesystem

The local filesystem provider stores artifacts in a local directory, making it a good fit for lightweight organizations or small teams that want a simpler setup while keeping proper versioning of compilation artifacts.

This storage is compatible with sharing through version control (commit the storage directory) or a shared drive.

Default configuration example:

```ts
storageConfiguration: {
  type: "local",
}
```

The `path` property can be optionally provided to specify the directory where the artifacts will be stored. By default, it is set to `ethoko-storage` within the project directory.

Use this provider when you want to keep the setup light and local while still tracking versions of artifacts across your team.
